context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 3


Problem 3.1 (20 pts)

Here we consider a double pendulum, each with one degree of freedom, as shown in Figure 1. Mass M1 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to a fixed origin. Its x-coordinate is X1 and it makes an angle θ1 with respect to the vertical (y-axis). Mass M2 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to mass M1. Its x-coordinate is X2 and it makes an angle θ2 with respect to the vertical direction.
You may assume that the 'hinges' at the origin and on mass M1 are frictionless. Gravity points downward in the y-direction. You may assume that all oscillations are small, i.e., that θ1 and θ2 are small and terms of order O(θ^2) are negligible.

[Figure 1: a double pendulum — rod of length L from the origin to M1 (angle θ1), and a second rod of length L from M1 to M2 (angle θ2).]

(a) Write down the equations of motion for the two oscillating masses.
(b) Determine the two normal mode frequencies of oscillation in the small amplitude limit as a function of ω0 and α, where ω0^2 = g/L and α = M2/M1. (You don't have to solve the corresponding amplitude ratios.)
(c) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → ∞? Do your results make sense?
(d) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → 0? Do your results make sense?


Problem 3.2 (20 pts)

Consider two identical LC circuits as shown in Figure 2. The two inductors are brought close together such that their mutual inductance M results in a coupling between the currents flowing in the two circuits.

[Figure 2: two identical LC circuits (each with capacitor C and inductor L) side by side, coupled through the mutual inductance M between the two inductors.]

(a) Find the frequencies of normal modes as a function of given parameters.
(b) What current patterns correspond to these normal modes? Could you use symmetry arguments to discover these modes?


Problem 3.3 (20 pts)

Consider three identical masses constrained to move on a frictionless circle. The masses are connected with identical springs each with spring constant k (see Figure 3). The circle is large such that you can ignore any effects related to the curvature. The circle is horizontal such that gravity can be ignored.

[Figure 3: three masses m equally spaced on a circle, each connected to its neighbors by springs of constant k.]

(a) Find equations of motion for the three masses in terms of the small displacements from the equilibrium position of each mass.
(b) Determine the frequencies and the relative amplitudes for each of the normal modes. Make a simple sketch of the motion of the masses for each of the normal modes. How many different frequencies are there in this system?
(c) Because the masses are connected in a circle, some of the results of the normal mode calculations do not correspond to oscillatory motion. Explain why.


Problem 3.4 (20 pts)

Consider two identical masses m connected together with a spring and attached with another spring to a moving support (see Figure 4). The support is oscillating vertically and its position is given by h(t) = A·cos(ωt). The Hooke constant of the two identical springs is k. Ignore effects of damping.

[Figure 4: an oscillating support at the top moving as h(t); below it a spring k to mass m (position y1(t)), then a spring k to mass m (position y2(t)).]

(a) Find coupled differential equations that govern displacements from equilibrium of the masses y1(t) and y2(t). Express your results in terms of ω0^2 = k/m. Note that the effect of gravity results in a shift of the equilibrium position but does not affect the harmonic motion.
(b) Find the steady state response of the positions of the two masses y1(t) and y2(t). Make a careful sketch of the amplitude as a function of the driving frequency ω for each of the masses.
(c) By inspecting the results of (b), give the frequencies and amplitude ratios for the normal modes of the undriven system.


Problem 3.5 (20 pts)

Two identical beads, each of mass m, are equally spaced along a massless string of length 3a (see Figure 5). Consider the system to be on a frictionless horizontal surface. Initially both ends of the string are fixed (Δ = 0). Assume that the string is under tension T at all times. Beads can execute small amplitude oscillations perpendicular to the string (displacements are exaggerated in the figure).

[Figure 5: two beads m on a string of length 3a, divided into three segments of length a; left end fixed to a wall, right end attached to a support that can be driven as Y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).]

(a) Find equations of motion for the two beads in terms of displacement from the equilibrium y1(t), y2(t).
(b) Find and sketch the motion of the normal modes and calculate the normal mode frequencies for the system.

Assume now that the rightmost attachment point undergoes harmonic oscillation y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).

(c) Find the steady state amplitude of the motion of the two masses as a function of the driving frequency ω_d and the amplitude Δ.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m trying to work through Problem Set 3 on normal modes, and I’m pretty lost on the double pendulum one. Could we start with Problem 3.1(a) and help me set up the small-angle equations of motion?
exchanges.tutor: Good place to start! Before writing any equations, tell me: what forces act on M1, and how do they differ from the forces acting on M2?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh on 3.1(a). Rather than scaffolding the setup for them, I should use the Socratic method to get them thinking about the physics first. The key starting point is identifying what approach to use (Newton's second law or Lagrangian) and what forces/torques act on each mass. I'll ask a single focused question to get them started without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: I think both masses feel gravity and tension, but M1 also feels some extra force from the second rod because M2 is hanging from it. I’m not fully sure how to separate the tensions though, especially since the rods are rigid.
exchanges.tutor: Good observation about M1 and M2 being different. Let's start with the simpler one: if you isolate M2 alone and draw its free body diagram, what forces act on it, and in which directions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the key asymmetry between M1 and M2, which is good. They're struggling with how to handle the tensions in rigid rods. Rather than explaining it, I should guide them to think about what Newton's second law looks like for each mass separately, starting with the simpler one (M2) to build intuition.

exchanges.student: For \(M_2\), I think there’s gravity downward and the tension in the second rod along the rod toward \(M_1\). For the tangential direction, I’m guessing only gravity contributes at small angle, so maybe \(M_2 L \ddot{\theta}_2 = -M_2 g \theta_2\)? But that feels too simple because it ignores that \(M_1\) is moving.
exchanges.tutor: Your force identification is right, but think carefully about this: the rod connecting M2 is attached to M1, which is itself moving. So when you write M2's acceleration, is it just L*θ̈2, or is something missing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with the forces on M2 but has made a common error: they're measuring θ2 relative to vertical, but the acceleration of M2 has two components — its own angular motion AND the motion of the pivot point M1. This is the key insight they need to discover. I should probe this without giving it away.

exchanges.student: Right, I think I forgot the motion of the pivot. So the tangential acceleration of \(M_2\) should include the acceleration of \(M_1\) plus the relative acceleration from \(\theta_2\). I’m not sure if that means something like \(x_2 \approx L\theta_1 + L\theta_2\), so then \(\ddot x_2 \approx L\ddot\theta_1 + L\ddot\theta_2\)?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — that's the correct expression for x2 and its acceleration. Now apply Newton's second law to M2 in the horizontal direction. What is the net horizontal force on M2 in the small-angle approximation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the x-coordinate of M2 in the small angle approximation and its second derivative. This is exactly right. I should affirm this and now push them to write the actual equation of motion for M2 using Newton's second law in the x-direction, applying the small angle approximation for the gravitational restoring force.

exchanges.student:
I think the horizontal force on \(M_2\) comes from gravity only indirectly, so it should really be the horizontal component of the rod tension. But if I use the small-angle relation with torque/ tangential motion, I think I get
\[
M_2(L\ddot\theta_1+L\ddot\theta_2) = -M_2 g\,\theta_2
\]
though I’m not totally confident about the sign.
exchanges.tutor: That's correct — good work on M2! Now tackle M1. It's more involved because M1 feels the pull from the second rod as well. What forces act on M1 in the horizontal direction?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the equation of motion for M2. The equation is right — the restoring force depends on θ2 (the angle the second rod makes with vertical, which determines the horizontal displacement of M2 relative to M1). I should confirm this and move them on to M1, which is more complex.

exchanges.student: For \(M_1\), I think there’s the horizontal component from the top rod tension, the horizontal component from the lower rod tension, and maybe gravity doesn’t matter directly in \(x\). I’m getting a bit tangled on whether the lower rod force on \(M_1\) is \(+M_2 g\theta_2\) or something involving \(\theta_1-\theta_2\).
exchanges.tutor: Think about Newton's third law: the second rod pulls on M2, but it also pulls on M1. How does the horizontal force the rod exerts on M1 relate to what you already found for M2?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close but getting confused about the reaction force from the second rod on M1. The key insight is Newton's third law: the force M2 exerts on M1 through the second rod is equal and opposite to the force the second rod exerts on M2. So whatever horizontal force the rod exerts on M2, M1 feels the opposite. I should guide them to this without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I think it should be equal and opposite to the horizontal force on \(M_2\) from that rod. So if on \(M_2\) the rod’s horizontal force was \(-M_2 g\theta_2\) in the small-angle limit, then on \(M_1\) it should be \(+M_2 g\theta_2\). Is that the right way to use it?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now combine that with the restoring force from the first rod on M1 (analogous to what you did for M2) and write the full equation of motion for M1.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly applied Newton's third law to find the reaction force on M1 from the second rod. Now they just need to combine this with the restoring force from the first rod to write M1's full equation of motion. I should affirm and push them to write the complete equation.

exchanges.student:
Then I think for \(M_1\),
\[
M_1 L\ddot\theta_1 = -M_1 g\theta_1 + M_2 g\theta_2.
\]
But I’m worried this still seems too simple, like maybe I’m missing an inertial coupling term from \(M_2\).
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — you are missing something. Think about it this way: M2 has a total acceleration (which you found earlier). By Newton's third law, the rod must exert a force on M2 to produce that acceleration. What does that imply about the force M2 exerts back on M1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is right to be suspicious — they are missing the inertial reaction force from M2 on M1. When M2 accelerates, it exerts a reaction force back on M1 through the second rod. This is separate from the gravitational (restoring) force. I should guide them to think about this through Newton's third law applied to the full acceleration of M2, not just the gravitational part.

exchanges.student: Oh, I see — I used only the gravity part for the rod force, but the rod force on \(M_2\) has to account for its full acceleration. So the back-force on \(M_1\) should involve something like \(M_2(L\ddot\theta_1+L\ddot\theta_2)\), not just \(M_2 g\theta_2\). I’m a little stuck on how to combine that with the first rod’s force cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: You have all the pieces now. The horizontal force the second rod exerts on M1 must account for M2's full acceleration — both the inertial and gravitational parts. Try writing the complete equation for M1 by combining all three contributions you've identified.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the missing inertial term. They now have all the pieces. I should encourage them to write out the full equation by combining what they have: the restoring force from rod 1 (-M1*g*θ1), the gravitational reaction from rod 2 (+M2*g*θ2), and the inertial reaction from M2's acceleration (-M2*L*(θ̈1+θ̈2)). This is a good moment to ask them to put it all together themselves rather than scaffolding further.

exchanges.student:
Okay, let me try: for \(M_1\), the horizontal acceleration is \(L\ddot{\theta}_1\). Then the horizontal forces are from the top rod, giving a restoring piece like \(-M_1 g \theta_1\), plus the back-force from the second rod, which I think should be \(-M_2(L\ddot{\theta}_1+L\ddot{\theta}_2)-M_2 g\theta_2\) with some sign choice I’m not confident about. So I’m not sure if the final equation is
\[
M_1 L\ddot{\theta}_1 = -M_1 g\theta_1 + M_2 g\theta_2 - M_2L(\ddot{\theta}_1+\ddot{\theta}_2)
\]
or if I messed up the signs.
exchanges.tutor: Your signs are correct — well done working through that carefully! You now have both equations of motion. Take a moment to write them together cleanly as a system. That's your answer to 3.1(a), and it sets up everything you need for parts (b)–(d).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the equation of motion for M1. The signs are right: the restoring force from rod 1 is -M1*g*θ1, the gravitational reaction from rod 2 is +M2*g*θ2 (since if θ2>0, M2 is displaced to the right and pulls M1 to the right), and the inertial reaction is -M2*L*(θ̈1+θ̈2). This is correct. I should affirm this and ask them to consolidate both equations together as a system, since that's what 3.1(a) asks for, and note we've used about 10 exchanges so should wrap up this part.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated excellent Socratic pedagogy throughout the conversation. Each turn posed a targeted question that guided the student to derive the next piece of the equation of motion themselves, without revealing answers prematurely. The tutor built incrementally on correct student contributions, maintained a supportive tone, kept responses concise, and stayed tightly anchored to the assignment. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a final answer or near-submission-ready output; every turn asked the student to reason through the next step (e.g., 'What forces act on M2?', 'What does that imply about the force M2 exerts back on M1?'). The student generated all key ideas and equations. No deductions for 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student progress (e.g., 'Exactly right', 'Good instinct') and built on it rather than restarting; no diagnostic failures were observed. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback at each step, pointing out what was missing and why (e.g., noting the missing inertial coupling term and explaining the Newton's third law reasoning needed). 2.1: The conversation progressed steadily without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The tutor stayed fully on-task with Problem 3.1(a) throughout. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive, typically one or two sentences plus a focused question. 3.2: Tone was consistently encouraging and formative, with no grading language.
